stem: Let \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{pmatrix} \) and \( v = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \). Given that \( v \) is an eigenvector of \( A \), what is its corresponding eigenvalue (i.e. the value of \( \lambda \) with \( Av = \lambda v \))?
solution: Every row of \( A \) sums to the same value here, so \( Av \) has both entries equal to that row sum: \( (Av)_1 = ([[a]])(1)+([[b]])(1) = [[answer]] \), and \( Av = [[answer]] v \), giving eigenvalue \( \lambda = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses the second row's entries; both rows give the same eigenvalue here, so this happens to coincide unless miscomputed, but is not how \( \lambda \) is read from row 1.
This is just the \( (1,1) \) entry of \( A \), not the eigenvalue.
This subtracts instead of adding the first row's entries.
This is off by one from the correct eigenvalue.
This is off by one from the correct eigenvalue.
This is off by two from the correct eigenvalue.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] \\ [[c]] & [[d]] \end{pmatrix} \) and \( v = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ -1 \end{pmatrix} \). Given that \( v \) is an eigenvector of \( A \), what is its corresponding eigenvalue?
solution: Row 1 gives \( (Av)_1 = ([[a]])(1) + ([[b]])(-1) = [[a]]-[[b]] = [[answer]] \), and since \( v \) is an eigenvector, \( Av = [[answer]] v \), so \( \lambda = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses row 2's entries in the same pattern; both rows are consistent with the same eigenvalue by construction, but \( \lambda \) is read directly from row 1's combination \( a-b \), not this expression.
This is just the \( (1,1) \) entry of \( A \), not the eigenvalue.
This adds instead of subtracting the first row's entries (using \( v=(1,1) \) instead of \( v=(1,-1) \)).
This is off by one from the correct eigenvalue.
This is off by one from the correct eigenvalue.
This is off by two from the correct eigenvalue.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a]] & [[b]] & [[c]] \\ [[d]] & [[e]] & [[f]] \\ [[g]] & [[h]] & [[i]] \end{pmatrix} \) and \( v = \begin{pmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \\ 1 \end{pmatrix} \). Given that \( v \) is an eigenvector of \( A \), what is its corresponding eigenvalue?
solution: Every row of \( A \) sums to the same value here, so \( (Av)_1 = [[a]]+[[b]]+[[c]] = [[answer]] \), and since \( v \) is an eigenvector, \( Av = [[answer]] v \), giving \( \lambda = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This uses row 2's entries; all three rows sum to the same eigenvalue by construction, but \( \lambda \) is read directly from row 1's sum here.
This multiplies the first row's entries instead of adding them.
This is just the \( (1,1) \) entry of \( A \), not the eigenvalue.
This is off by one from the correct eigenvalue.
This is off by one from the correct eigenvalue.
This is off by two from the correct eigenvalue.

stem: Let \( A = \begin{pmatrix} [[a11]] & [[a12]] \\ [[a21]] & [[a22]] \end{pmatrix} \) and \( v = \begin{pmatrix} [[p]] \\ [[q]] \end{pmatrix} \). Given that \( v \) is an eigenvector of \( A \), use the SECOND row of \( Av \) to find the corresponding eigenvalue \( \lambda \) (i.e. compute \( (Av)_2 / v_2 \)).
solution: Row 2 of \( Av \) is \( ([[a21]])([[p]]) + ([[a22]])([[q]]) \); dividing by \( v_2=[[q]] \) gives \( \lambda = [[answer]] \) (matching \( \lambda=[[lam]] \) used to build \( A \)).
distractor_rationales: This computes the ratio from row 1 instead of row 2 as asked (both give the same \( \lambda \), but this isn't the row-2 computation requested).
This is just the \( (2,2) \) entry of \( A \), not the ratio \( (Av)_2/v_2 \).
This adds the second row's entries directly instead of forming the weighted combination with \( v \) and dividing by \( v_2 \).
This is off by one from the correct eigenvalue.
This is off by one from the correct eigenvalue.
This is off by two from the correct eigenvalue.
